clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of bronchiectasis presents with progressive bilateral leg swelling and frothy urine. He has a history of three admissions for pneumonia in the past year. Urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria. Serum albumin is 18 g/L. A renal biopsy is performed and Congo red staining shows apple-green birefringence under polarised light. Which of the following best describes this complication?
options: Primary AL amyloidosis due to plasma cell dyscrasia
Secondary AA amyloidosis due to chronic suppurative infection
Membranous nephropathy due to immune complex deposition
Minimal change disease triggered by recurrent infection
IgA nephropathy associated with respiratory tract infection
explanation: Secondary AA amyloidosis is a recognised long-term complication of bronchiectasis. Chronic suppurative infection leads to sustained acute-phase response with persistently elevated serum amyloid A (SAA) protein. Over years this deposits in organs — predominantly kidneys — causing nephrotic syndrome. Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarised light confirms amyloid deposition. AA amyloidosis is the specific type seen with chronic inflammatory/infectious conditions, not AL (which arises from plasma cell clones).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Primary AL amyloidosis arises from abnormal plasma cell clones producing immunoglobulin light chains. There is no evidence of myeloma or MGUS here; the cause is chronic infection.
Correct. Chronic bronchiectasis with recurrent suppurative infections drives sustained SAA elevation, resulting in AA amyloid deposition in kidneys.
Membranous nephropathy involves subepithelial immune complex deposition, most commonly associated with hepatitis B, malignancy, or autoimmune disease — not bronchiectasis.
Minimal change disease causes nephrotic syndrome but is associated with allergic conditions and lymphoma, not chronic suppurative lung disease.
IgA nephropathy can follow upper respiratory infections but presents with haematuria and mild proteinuria after acute illness, not as a long-term complication of bronchiectasis.
clinicalPearl: Amyloidosis (AA type) is a recognised but rare systemic complication of long-standing bronchiectasis; nephrotic syndrome in a bronchiectasis patient with chronic infection warrants renal biopsy with Congo red staining.
keyClue: 15-year bronchiectasis + nephrotic syndrome + Congo red positive biopsy = secondary AA amyloidosis.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse AA amyloidosis (chronic infection/inflammation) with AL amyloidosis (plasma cell dyscrasia). Congo red positivity alone does not distinguish them — the clinical context does.
missedConcept: Bronchiectasis causes systemic complications beyond the lung: amyloidosis, metastatic abscesses, and cor pulmonale are all recognised.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with established bronchiectasis attends her annual review. Her sputum culture this year has grown Pseudomonas aeruginosa for the first time. She is currently well, with no fever, and her FEV1 is 68% predicted. She has had two exacerbations in the past 12 months. Which of the following statements most accurately describes the prognostic implication of this finding?
options: Pseudomonas isolation is a transient finding and does not change long-term prognosis
Pseudomonas colonisation is associated with more frequent exacerbations and a worse prognosis
Pseudomonas isolation only affects prognosis if the patient develops bacteraemia
Pseudomonas colonisation exclusively worsens prognosis in cystic fibrosis, not non-CF bronchiectasis
Pseudomonas isolation triggers automatic referral for lung transplant assessment
explanation: Chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation in bronchiectasis is a well-established marker of worse prognosis. It is associated with more frequent exacerbations, faster lung function decline, poorer quality of life, and increased mortality. Eradication should be attempted on first isolation using inhaled ciprofloxacin or tobramycin combined with a 3-week course of oral ciprofloxacin. Once chronic colonisation is established, long-term inhaled antibiotics (e.g. tobramycin) are used to suppress bacterial load.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pseudomonas colonisation is not typically transient; without active eradication, most patients progress to chronic colonisation, which carries significant prognostic weight.
Correct. Pseudomonas colonisation in non-CF bronchiectasis predicts more frequent exacerbations, accelerated lung function decline, and increased mortality.
Bacteraemia is not required for Pseudomonas to worsen prognosis; the chronic airway inflammatory burden itself drives deterioration.
The adverse prognostic impact of Pseudomonas colonisation applies to both cystic fibrosis and non-CF bronchiectasis.
Pseudomonas isolation does not automatically trigger transplant referral; the first step is eradication attempt followed by long-term inhaled antibiotic suppression if eradication fails.
clinicalPearl: First Pseudomonas isolation in bronchiectasis warrants an eradication attempt. Chronic colonisation independently predicts worse clinical outcomes.
keyClue: First isolation of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in bronchiectasis = attempt eradication now; if established, inhaled tobramycin for long-term suppression.
commonExamTrap: Candidates may think Pseudomonas only matters in cystic fibrosis. It has equivalent prognostic significance in non-CF bronchiectasis.
missedConcept: Microbiology in bronchiectasis determines prognosis and guides therapy. Pseudomonas colonisation shifts management toward suppressive inhaled antibiotics.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman with CT-confirmed bilateral bronchiectasis has had four infective exacerbations in the past 12 months, each requiring antibiotics. She performs chest physiotherapy twice daily and uses nebulised hypertonic saline. Her sputum cultures have never grown Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Her FEV1 is 72% predicted. Hearing tests are normal and a recent ECG shows a normal QTc interval. What is the most appropriate next step in her long-term management?
options: Commence long-term inhaled corticosteroids to reduce airway inflammation
Commence long-term oral azithromycin three times per week
Refer immediately for lung transplant assessment
Commence prophylactic oral amoxicillin-clavulanate daily
Commence nebulised N-acetylcysteine to improve mucociliary clearance
explanation: Long-term macrolide therapy (azithromycin 250–500 mg three times weekly) is recommended for patients with bronchiectasis who have three or more exacerbations per year despite optimised airway clearance. Azithromycin reduces exacerbation frequency through its immunomodulatory and weak antibacterial properties. Before starting, it is essential to exclude non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) infection (macrolide monotherapy would induce resistance), check QTc (macrolides prolong QT), test hearing (ototoxicity risk), and check liver function. Pseudomonas colonisation would instead prompt inhaled tobramycin.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Inhaled corticosteroids have no established role in bronchiectasis management and are not recommended for reducing exacerbation frequency.
Correct. Azithromycin three times weekly is the evidence-based macrolide prophylaxis for patients with ≥3 exacerbations/year who are already on optimised airway clearance.
Lung transplant referral is not appropriate at this stage; the patient has preserved lung function and has not yet been offered macrolide prophylaxis.
Beta-lactam prophylaxis (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanate) is not a guideline-recommended strategy for bronchiectasis exacerbation prevention.
N-acetylcysteine has not demonstrated benefit in reducing exacerbations in bronchiectasis and is not a recommended therapy.
clinicalPearl: Azithromycin three times weekly reduces exacerbations in bronchiectasis. Always exclude NTM before starting and monitor QTc and hearing.
keyClue: ≥3 exacerbations/year + on physiotherapy + non-Pseudomonas = long-term oral azithromycin.
commonExamTrap: Candidates prescribe macrolides without checking for NTM or QTc. Both must be done before starting azithromycin in bronchiectasis.
missedConcept: Macrolide prophylaxis works via immunomodulation in bronchiectasis, not just antibacterial effect. Inhaled tobramycin is reserved for Pseudomonas colonisation.

clinicalStem: A 43-year-old man with a 10-year history of bilateral bronchiectasis presents to the emergency department after coughing up approximately 400 mL of bright red blood over 4 hours. He is haemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, heart rate 94 bpm, and oxygen saturation of 96% on room air. His last sputum culture grew Pseudomonas aeruginosa. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for his haemoptysis?
options: Urgent surgical resection of the affected bronchiectatic lobe
Intravenous tranexamic acid infusion as first-line haemostatic therapy
Bronchial artery embolisation
Fibreoptic bronchoscopy with endobronchial tamponade
High-dose intravenous co-amoxiclav to treat underlying infection
explanation: Massive haemoptysis in bronchiectasis (typically >100–300 mL/24 hours) arises from hypertrophied bronchial arteries supplying areas of chronic inflammation. Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE) is the treatment of choice for massive haemoptysis in bronchiectasis — it is minimally invasive, effective in the acute setting, and controls bleeding in approximately 75–90% of cases. Surgery may be considered if embolisation fails or is not technically feasible, but carries high operative risk in bilateral disease. Tranexamic acid may reduce minor haemoptysis but is not the definitive treatment for massive haemoptysis. Bronchoscopy can identify the bleeding site and temporise but does not address the underlying vascular anatomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgical resection is reserved for cases where BAE has failed or is contraindicated, or when the disease is localised to one lobe with good contralateral lung function. It carries high morbidity in bilateral bronchiectasis.
Tranexamic acid may be useful for mild haemoptysis but is not established as definitive treatment for massive haemoptysis.
Correct. Bronchial artery embolisation is the treatment of choice for massive haemoptysis in bronchiectasis — safe, effective, and preserves lung tissue.
Endobronchial tamponade via bronchoscopy is a temporising measure, not definitive therapy.
Antibiotics address the underlying infection but do not control acute haemorrhage from hypertrophied bronchial arteries.
clinicalPearl: Massive haemoptysis in bronchiectasis = bronchial artery embolisation as first-line definitive treatment. Surgery is the fallback when BAE fails.
keyClue: Large-volume haemoptysis + bronchiectasis + haemodynamically stable = bronchial artery embolisation.
commonExamTrap: Students choose surgery first. BAE is the preferred first intervention because it is less invasive and highly effective even in bilateral disease.
missedConcept: In bronchiectasis, massive haemoptysis originates from hypertrophied bronchial arteries (systemic circulation), not pulmonary arteries — hence embolisation targets the bronchial circulation.
redFlagReason: Massive haemoptysis is a life-threatening emergency. Delay in bronchial artery embolisation risks exsanguination and asphyxiation.

subtopic: Pulmonary hypertension as a prognostic marker
clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man with biopsy-confirmed idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) has been on pirfenidone for 14 months. His most recent 6-minute walk test distance has declined by 55 metres. A transthoracic echocardiogram ordered to evaluate exertional dyspnoea reveals an estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 52 mmHg, consistent with moderate pulmonary hypertension. His FVC is 64% predicted. Which of the following statements best describes the significance of this new finding?
options: Pulmonary hypertension in IPF is entirely reversible with supplemental oxygen therapy
Pulmonary hypertension in IPF responds well to phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, improving both symptoms and mortality
The development of pulmonary hypertension in IPF is a benign adaptive response to hypoxaemia
Pulmonary hypertension in IPF is associated with increased mortality and disease progression
Pulmonary hypertension indicates the patient should stop pirfenidone and switch to nintedanib
explanation: Pulmonary hypertension (PH) complicates approximately 30–50% of patients with IPF and is one of the recognised independent predictors of increased mortality. It develops due to hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and vascular remodelling in fibrotic lung tissue. Other markers of increased mortality in IPF include older age at diagnosis, severe FVC impairment, significant hypoxaemia, honeycombing on HRCT, and a >10% relative decline in FVC over 12 months. PH in IPF does not reliably respond to pulmonary vasodilators (unlike PAH), and switching antifibrotics is not indicated solely because PH has developed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oxygen corrects hypoxic vasoconstriction and may prevent worsening, but PH in established IPF is not fully reversible.
PDE-5 inhibitors (e.g. sildenafil) have not demonstrated consistent mortality benefit in IPF-related PH. Unlike primary PAH, the vascular changes are tied to parenchymal fibrosis.
Pulmonary hypertension in IPF is not benign — it is a recognised marker of worse prognosis and increased 12-month mortality.
Correct. PH in IPF is an independent predictor of increased mortality and reflects advanced, progressive disease.
There is no indication to switch antifibrotic therapy based on the development of PH alone.
clinicalPearl: PH complicates up to 50% of IPF patients and is an independent mortality predictor. Echocardiogram is part of the IPF complication surveillance workup.
keyClue: IPF + new pulmonary hypertension on echo = worse prognosis, increased mortality.
commonExamTrap: Candidates may apply PAH vasodilator logic to IPF-related PH. Pulmonary vasodilators do not reliably improve outcomes in IPF-associated PH.
missedConcept: IPF prognostic factors include FVC decline >10%, DLco decline >15%, hypoxaemia, honeycombing, pulmonary hypertension, and older age.

clinicalStem: A 71-year-old woman with UIP pattern on HRCT and a surgical lung biopsy confirming IPF is commenced on a new antifibrotic medication. Her doctor explains that this drug works by inhibiting multiple receptor tyrosine kinases involved in fibroblast proliferation and differentiation, including VEGFR, PDGFR, and FGFR. The drug is associated with diarrhoea as its most common side effect, and liver function should be monitored. Six months later, her FVC decline is 2% relative to baseline. Which drug has been prescribed?
options: Pirfenidone
Nintedanib
Prednisolone
N-acetylcysteine
Sildenafil
explanation: Nintedanib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor that targets VEGFR (vascular endothelial growth factor receptor), PDGFR (platelet-derived growth factor receptor), and FGFR (fibroblast growth factor receptor). These receptors are involved in the fibroblast-to-myofibroblast differentiation and proliferation that drives IPF progression. Nintedanib slows FVC decline by approximately 2–4% at one year relative to placebo. Its most common side effect is diarrhoea (occurs in >60%), with hepatotoxicity requiring LFT monitoring. Pirfenidone has a different mechanism (anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic via TGF-beta suppression) and has myalgia, photosensitivity, and GI side effects.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pirfenidone is an anti-inflammatory/antifibrotic agent that acts via multiple pathways including TGF-beta suppression. Its main side effects are photosensitivity, rash, nausea, and fatigue — not primarily diarrhoea.
Correct. Nintedanib is the multiple-receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR/PDGFR/FGFR with diarrhoea as its main side effect.
Prednisolone (immunosuppressant/corticosteroid) is actually harmful in IPF — it does not slow progression and increases mortality when combined with azathioprine and NAC.
N-acetylcysteine was trialled in IPF but shown to be ineffective and potentially harmful in the PANTHER-IPF trial.
Sildenafil is a PDE-5 inhibitor used in pulmonary arterial hypertension. It is not an approved antifibrotic for IPF.
clinicalPearl: Nintedanib = multiple receptor TKI (VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR). Main side effect = diarrhoea. Slows FVC decline 2–4% at 1 year.
keyClue: TKI inhibiting VEGFR + PDGFR + FGFR + diarrhoea as main side effect = nintedanib.
commonExamTrap: Confusing pirfenidone and nintedanib mechanisms. Pirfenidone is not a TKI; it works via anti-inflammatory pathways including TGF-beta. Nintedanib is the TKI.
missedConcept: Both pirfenidone and nintedanib slow FVC decline by similar amounts (2–4%) but have distinct mechanisms and side effect profiles.

clinicalStem: A 64-year-old man with known IPF on nintedanib presents with a 10-day history of worsening dyspnoea and a new cough. His oxygen saturation is 84% on room air. Chest HRCT shows new bilateral ground-glass opacities superimposed on the known UIP fibrosis pattern. BAL and sputum cultures are negative. Blood cultures are negative. Procalcitonin is 0.2 ng/mL. An infectious cause has been excluded. He is commenced on high-dose corticosteroids for presumed acute exacerbation of IPF (AE-IPF). You are asked to counsel the patient's family regarding prognosis. Which statement is most accurate?
options: AE-IPF carries an in-hospital mortality of approximately 10% with high-dose steroids
AE-IPF carries an in-hospital mortality of approximately 50–90%, even with treatment
AE-IPF resolves completely in most patients if steroids are started within 48 hours
AE-IPF median survival after the episode is 5–7 years with continued antifibrotic therapy
AE-IPF is only triggered by infection and carries a better prognosis when infection is excluded
explanation: Acute exacerbation of IPF (AE-IPF) is a catastrophic, life-threatening event defined as acute respiratory worsening (within 30 days) with new bilateral GGOs on HRCT superimposed on UIP background, with no identifiable cause (infection, heart failure, PE excluded). The in-hospital mortality is 50–90%, making it one of the most lethal complications in respiratory medicine. Median survival after an AE-IPF episode is only 3–4 months. High-dose corticosteroids are used empirically but have no robust evidence base, and their effect on mortality is uncertain. Lung transplant remains the only potentially curative option for IPF but is rarely feasible acutely.
wrongAnswerExplanations: In-hospital mortality is not 10%; it is 50–90%. This is a critical distinction for family counselling and clinical decision-making.
Correct. AE-IPF in-hospital mortality is 50–90%, and median survival after the episode is approximately 3–4 months.
AE-IPF does not resolve completely. It typically leads to a stepwise acceleration of the underlying fibrosis with permanent loss of lung function in survivors.
Post-AE-IPF median survival is 3–4 months, not 5–7 years. Antifibrotic continuation does not restore that prognosis.
AE-IPF is defined as acute worsening with no identifiable cause. Excluding infection does not confer a better prognosis — all AE-IPF carries very high mortality.
clinicalPearl: AE-IPF: in-hospital mortality 50–90%; median post-episode survival 3–4 months. Counsel families early about the gravity of this diagnosis.
keyClue: New bilateral GGOs superimposed on UIP + infection excluded + rapid deterioration = AE-IPF; mortality 50–90%.
commonExamTrap: Students underestimate AE-IPF mortality. The prognosis is far worse than most acute respiratory conditions and rivals that of many malignancies.
missedConcept: AE-IPF is distinct from infection-triggered respiratory deterioration. Even when precipitated by a trigger, the underlying mechanism is diffuse alveolar damage on fibrotic lung.
redFlagReason: AE-IPF carries 50-90% in-hospital mortality. Failure to recognise it and counsel appropriately constitutes a dangerous knowledge gap.

clinicalStem: A 66-year-old woman has been newly diagnosed with IPF based on typical UIP pattern on HRCT and a compatible clinical presentation. She is commenced on nintedanib. She asks her respiratory physician how often she should attend for follow-up and what tests will be done at each visit. Which of the following monitoring schedules is most consistent with current guidelines for IPF?
options: Annual chest X-ray and spirometry only, unless symptoms worsen
PFTs and 6-minute walk test every 4–6 months, with echocardiogram if PH is suspected
CT chest every 3 months to assess fibrosis progression
Bronchoscopy with BAL every 6 months to monitor for superimposed infection
Monthly clinic visits with full blood count and inflammatory markers only
explanation: Current IPF management guidelines recommend pulmonary function tests (FVC, DLco) and 6-minute walk tests (6MWT) every 4–6 months to monitor disease progression. A >10% relative decline in FVC or >15% decline in DLco over 12 months is a clinically significant threshold associated with worse prognosis and may prompt transplant referral reassessment. Echocardiography is indicated if pulmonary hypertension is suspected clinically. Annual CXR and spirometry alone are insufficient. Serial CT is used selectively, not as routine surveillance every 3 months. BAL is not part of routine follow-up in established IPF.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Annual CXR and spirometry alone are inadequate. PFTs every 4–6 months are needed to detect significant FVC/DLco decline that changes management.
Correct. PFTs and 6MWT every 4–6 months are the recommended monitoring intervals; echocardiogram is added if PH is clinically suspected.
CT chest every 3 months is excessive radiation exposure and not recommended for routine follow-up; CT is used for clinical change or specific indications.
Routine BAL is not part of established IPF follow-up; it is reserved for cases where infection or an alternative diagnosis is being considered.
Monthly full blood count alone is inadequate to assess IPF progression; functional and physiological monitoring is essential.
clinicalPearl: IPF monitoring: PFTs + 6MWT every 4–6 months. A >10% FVC decline at 12 months = significant progression and poor prognosis.
keyClue: IPF follow-up = PFTs (FVC, DLco) + 6MWT every 4–6 months.
commonExamTrap: Annual review is too infrequent for IPF. Disease can progress significantly within months; 4–6 monthly monitoring captures meaningful decline.
missedConcept: The 6MWT desaturation and distance correlate with exercise capacity and survival in IPF, making it a key monitoring tool alongside spirometry.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man is admitted with community-acquired pneumonia. Chest X-ray reveals a right-sided pleural effusion. Diagnostic thoracocentesis is performed. Pleural fluid analysis shows: appearance cloudy/turbid, pH 7.15, glucose 1.8 mmol/L (serum glucose 6.1 mmol/L), LDH 820 U/L, protein 48 g/L, Gram-positive cocci in chains on Gram stain. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Repeat thoracocentesis in 48 hours and continue IV antibiotics
Commence intrapleural fibrinolytics without drainage and reassess in 72 hours
Insert an intercostal chest drain and commence IV antibiotics
Commence oral antibiotics and discharge with outpatient follow-up
Urgent referral for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) as first-line intervention
explanation: This pleural fluid profile — pH <7.2, low glucose, high LDH, turbid appearance, and positive Gram stain — is consistent with a complicated parapneumonic effusion or frank empyema. These effusions will not resolve with antibiotics alone and require drainage. Intercostal chest drain (ICD) insertion is the first-line intervention, guided by ultrasound. IV antibiotics targeting streptococcal species (Gram-positive cocci in chains) should be started concurrently. Intrapleural fibrinolytics (e.g. tPA + DNase) may be added if the effusion is loculated and not draining adequately. VATS is reserved for cases where ICD and fibrinolytics fail. Simple repeat aspiration is inadequate for empyema.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Repeat thoracocentesis alone is inadequate for complicated effusion or empyema — drainage via ICD is required. Delay risks sepsis and pleural thickening.
Intrapleural fibrinolytics alone without a drain in situ are not appropriate first-line management; drainage must be established first.
Correct. ICD insertion + IV antibiotics is the definitive first-line management for complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema with pH <7.2 and positive Gram stain.
Oral antibiotics and discharge are appropriate only for uncomplicated (free-flowing, pH >7.2, sterile) effusions — not for empyema with positive Gram stain.
VATS is reserved for failure of medical management (ICD + fibrinolytics), not as an initial intervention.
clinicalPearl: Pleural fluid pH <7.2 + positive Gram stain = complicated effusion/empyema = chest drain + IV antibiotics, not aspiration alone.
keyClue: pH 7.15 + low glucose + turbid + Gram stain positive = empyema = ICD insertion.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose repeat aspiration or antibiotics alone. These are insufficient once pH drops below 7.2 or organisms are identified on Gram stain.
missedConcept: Light's criteria exudate vs transudate distinction does not drive management here — the pH and microbiology do. pH <7.2 mandates drainage regardless of other parameters.
redFlagReason: Untreated empyema progresses to sepsis, fibrothorax, and death. Drainage with chest drain is time-critical.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old woman with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer has had three large right-sided pleural effusions drained by therapeutic thoracocentesis over 6 weeks. Each drainage has provided symptomatic relief but the effusion recurs within 10–14 days. She is not fit for systemic chemotherapy. Performance status is ECOG 2. She asks about options to avoid repeated thoracocentesis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her long-term management?
options: Diuretics to prevent reaccumulation of the effusion
Systemic bevacizumab to reduce VEGF-driven pleural fluid production
Talc pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) placement
Pleurectomy as definitive surgery for malignant effusion
Commence prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent secondary infection
explanation: Recurrent malignant pleural effusion (MPE) requiring repeated drainage is a common palliative challenge. The two established options are: (1) chemical pleurodesis (talc most effective) via an ICD or thoracoscopy to obliterate the pleural space, and (2) indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) for ambulatory, home-based intermittent drainage. IPC is particularly suited to patients with poor performance status or short prognosis who cannot tolerate pleurodesis procedure, and those with a non-expandable lung (trapped lung). Both approaches have comparable symptom control in trials. Diuretics are ineffective for malignant exudates. Bevacizumab is not an established treatment for MPE. Pleurectomy is a surgical procedure with high morbidity, not appropriate for ECOG 2 patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Diuretics are used for transudates (e.g. heart failure, cirrhosis). They do not prevent reaccumulation of malignant exudative effusions.
Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) is not approved or routinely used for controlling malignant pleural effusion specifically, although VEGF does contribute to effusion formation.
Correct. Talc pleurodesis or IPC are the two evidence-based options for recurrent MPE. IPC is preferred in poor performance status or non-expandable lung.
Pleurectomy (surgical decortication) is a major operation not appropriate for patients with metastatic disease and ECOG 2 performance status.
Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated in the management of recurrent malignant effusion and carry risks of resistance and side effects.
clinicalPearl: Recurrent MPE: talc pleurodesis (if lung expandable) or IPC (if trapped lung or poor performance status) are the two established options.
keyClue: Recurrent malignant effusion requiring repeat thoracocenteses + poor performance status = indwelling pleural catheter.
commonExamTrap: Diuretics are frequently selected but are not effective for exudative malignant effusions. Only transudates (cardiac/hepatic/nephrotic) respond to diuresis.
missedConcept: Trapped lung (non-expandable lung after drainage) precludes effective pleurodesis. IPC is the preferred approach in this scenario.

clinicalStem: A 61-year-old man with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents with a 3-week history of progressive facial swelling, redness, and swelling of both arms. He reports headache that worsens when bending forward. On examination, there is visible distension of neck veins and superficial chest wall veins. CT chest reveals a right upper lobe mass with mediastinal involvement compressing the superior vena cava. What is this syndrome, and what histological type is most commonly responsible?
options: Pancoast syndrome; most commonly caused by adenocarcinoma of the apex
Superior vena cava syndrome; most commonly caused by small cell lung cancer or squamous cell carcinoma
Horner's syndrome; most commonly caused by small cell lung cancer at the pulmonary apex
Eaton-Lambert syndrome; most commonly caused by small cell lung cancer
Trousseau syndrome; most commonly caused by adenocarcinoma
explanation: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome results from external compression or invasion of the SVC, causing venous obstruction and the classic triad of facial oedema, arm swelling, and dilated collateral chest wall veins. It is a recognised oncological emergency. Lung cancer is the most common cause (approximately 75% of cases), with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and squamous cell carcinoma being the most frequent culprits due to their central location and tendency to invade mediastinal structures. Management includes urgent corticosteroids to reduce tumour-related inflammation, radiotherapy or stenting, and systemic treatment of the underlying malignancy. Pancoast syndrome (apical tumour causing shoulder/arm pain + Horner's) is a distinct entity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pancoast syndrome affects the apex of the lung (sulcus superior tumour) causing brachial plexus invasion, rib erosion, and Horner's syndrome. It does not cause facial and bilateral arm swelling with chest vein distension.
Correct. SVC syndrome from central lung cancer (SCLC or squamous cell) causes facial oedema, arm swelling, and collateral vein distension — an oncological emergency.
Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) is caused by cervical sympathetic chain disruption by apical tumours. It does not cause bilateral facial and arm swelling.
Eaton-Lambert syndrome is a paraneoplastic neuromuscular junction disorder (proximal muscle weakness that improves with repetition) associated with SCLC. It is not the diagnosis here.
Trousseau syndrome is migratory thrombophlebitis and a hypercoagulable state associated with mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma. Not the correct diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: SVC syndrome = facial swelling + arm oedema + dilated chest wall veins. Lung cancer (SCLC or SCC) is the most common cause. This is an oncological emergency.
keyClue: Facial swelling + arm swelling + dilated chest veins + mediastinal mass = SVC syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse SVC syndrome with Pancoast syndrome or Horner's syndrome. Each has a distinct anatomical and clinical presentation.
missedConcept: SVC syndrome may be the initial presentation of previously undiagnosed lung cancer. Prompt CT and tissue diagnosis with urgent oncology referral are essential.
redFlagReason: SVC syndrome is an oncological emergency. Airway oedema and laryngeal oedema can cause life-threatening obstruction if untreated.

clinicalStem: A 63-year-old ex-smoker presents with haemoptysis and weight loss. CT chest reveals a 4.5 cm right lower lobe mass with ipsilateral mediastinal lymph node involvement (station 4R). PET-CT shows no distant metastases. CT-guided biopsy confirms non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma). His ECOG performance status is 1. Based on TNM 8th edition staging, what stage is this tumour and what is the approximate 5-year survival?
options: Stage IIB (T3N1M0); 5-year survival approximately 53%
Stage IVA (T2bN3M1a); 5-year survival less than 5%
Stage IIIA (T3N2M0 or T2bN2M0); 5-year survival approximately 10–35%
Stage IA (T1bN0M0); 5-year survival approximately 92%
Stage IIB (T2bN1M0); 5-year survival approximately 53%
explanation: A 4.5 cm tumour is classified as T2b (>4 cm and ≤5 cm) under TNM 8th edition. Ipsilateral mediastinal lymph node involvement (station 4R = ipsilateral mediastinal/paratracheal) corresponds to N2 disease. No distant metastases = M0. Therefore stage = T2bN2M0 = Stage IIIA. Stage IIIA NSCLC has a 5-year survival of approximately 10–35%, depending on resectability and treatment. It is generally treated with concurrent chemoradiotherapy +/- durvalumab consolidation. N2 disease makes surgical resection controversial and often not first-line. N3 would be contralateral or supraclavicular, which would be Stage IIIB.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Stage IIB includes T3N1M0 (ipsilateral hilar nodes, not mediastinal). N2 (ipsilateral mediastinal) disease cannot be Stage IIB.
Stage IVA requires distant metastasis (M1a = contralateral lung nodules or pleural/pericardial disease). This patient has no distant disease.
Correct. T2b (4.5 cm) + N2 (ipsilateral mediastinal nodes) + M0 = Stage IIIA. 5-year survival approximately 10–35%.
Stage IA requires T1 (<3 cm) and N0 — neither applies here. This patient has a 4.5 cm tumour and N2 disease.
Stage IIB T2bN1M0 requires ipsilateral hilar (not mediastinal) node involvement. Station 4R is a mediastinal station = N2, not N1.
clinicalPearl: N2 disease (ipsilateral mediastinal nodes) = Stage III NSCLC. Hilar nodes (N1) = Stage II. Ipsilateral mediastinal nodes push to Stage IIIA, contralateral = IIIB.
keyClue: 4.5 cm tumour + ipsilateral mediastinal lymph node + no metastasis = T2bN2M0 = Stage IIIA NSCLC.
commonExamTrap: Candidates confuse N1 (ipsilateral hilar) and N2 (ipsilateral mediastinal) nodes, leading to incorrect staging. Mediastinal = N2 = Stage III.
missedConcept: TNM 8th edition changes: T2 split into T2a (3-4 cm) and T2b (4-5 cm). Accurate T-category sizing matters for correct staging.
